title: Question of Blanco, Cook, Hatzel, Hilaire, Illingworth, and McCarty
statement_text: Is it true that every connected graph $G$ has a tree decomposition whose indexing tree is a subgraph of $G$, and whose width is bounded by some function of the pathwidth of $G$?
context_text: Blanco et al. (2024) answered Dvořák's original question negatively by showing that no such tree decomposition exists with width bounded by a function of the treewidth — not even if the indexing tree is only required to be a minor of $G$. They observed that their counterexamples have bounded pathwidth (treewidth 2 but unbounded pathwidth) and proposed the relaxed question replacing treewidth by pathwidth.
attributed_to: Blanco, Cook, Hatzel, Hilaire, Illingworth, and McCarty
paper_contribution: Answers the question positively: proves (Theorem 1) that every connected graph $G$ of pathwidth less than $k$ has a tree decomposition of width at most $f(k)$ indexed by a spanning tree $T$ of $G$ in which every vertex belongs to its own bag, where $f:\mathbb{N}\to\mathbb{N}$ is an explicit function.
paper_title: Tree decompositions whose trees are subgraphs: An application of Simon's factorization
